21.name: FTP
21.description: File Transfer Protocol (FTP) is a standard protocol used for the transfer of computer files between a client and server.
21.whyAttackersCare: FTP servers frequently contain sensitive configuration backups, cleartext credentials, or proprietary documents. Legacy versions are vulnerable to RCE, and anonymous logins are common.
21.recon: Anonymous login testing, directory traversal checking, banner grabbing for software versions.
21.exploit: Brute-forcing accounts, executing known version exploits (like vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor), using write privileges to plant Web Shells.
21.detection: Multiple login failures from single IP; FTP sessions established by anonymous users from external subnets.

22.name: SSH
22.description: Secure Shell (SSH) is a cryptographic network protocol for operating network services securely over an unsecured network.
22.whyAttackersCare: SSH provides direct, encrypted terminal command-line access to the host. Compromising credentials guarantees an active foothold on the machine.
22.recon: Banner version scanning, SSH authentication method enumeration.
22.exploit: Credential spraying targeting admin accounts, private key theft from developer configurations.
22.objective: Initial Access, Command Execution, Persistence, Lateral Movement.
22.detection: High volume of port 22 connection attempts; successful logins from anomalous geographical regions.

23.name: Telnet
23.description: Telnet is a legacy terminal emulation protocol providing bidirectional interactive text communication in cleartext.
23.whyAttackersCare: Telnet transmits all packets (including usernames and passwords) in cleartext, exposing credentials to simple network sniffing.
23.recon: Interactive connection attempts to capture login banners.
23.exploit: Credential sniffing via MitM sniffing, automated default credentials dictionary scans on embedded devices.
23.objective: Initial Access, Credential Sniffing, Device Control.
23.detection: Any active Telnet session traversing external boundaries.

25.name: SMTP
25.description: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP) is the internet standard for electronic mail transmission.
25.whyAttackersCare: Open mail relays can be exploited to distribute phishing campaigns or spam. Legacy user enumeration commands (VRFY/EXPN) leak system usernames.
25.recon: VRFY/EXPN commands execution, relay routing permission testing.
25.exploit: Email harvesting to build target lists, phishing campaign distribution.
25.detection: High volume of emails sent to external domains from non-relay servers.

53.name: DNS
53.description: Domain Name System (DNS) translates human-readable domain names to numerical IP addresses.
53.whyAttackersCare: Open zone transfers leak the entire internal network layout, hostname mappings, and active server IPs, facilitating reconnaissance.
53.recon: Zone transfer requests (AXFR queries), subdomain dictionary lookups.
53.exploit: DNS cache poisoning, DNS amplification DDoS vectors.
53.detection: AXFR zone transfer query requests originating from external IP networks.

80.name: HTTP
80.description: Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is the foundation of data communication for the World Wide Web.
80.whyAttackersCare: Web applications are highly visible and prone to custom logic flaws, including SQL injection, path traversals, and access bypasses.
80.recon: Directory brute-forcing (Gobuster), web vulnerability scanning, banner extraction.
80.exploit: SQL Injection, local/remote file inclusions (LFI/RFI), uploading Web Shell backdoors.
80.objective: Initial Access, Web Shell Execution, Data Extraction.
80.detection: Spikes in 404 response errors associated with automated directory scans.

443.name: HTTPS
443.description: Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure (HTTPS) encrypts standard web traffic using TLS/SSL.
443.whyAttackersCare: Encrypts web application exploits, making detection difficult for basic network intrusion systems. Prone to SSL cipher vulnerabilities.
443.recon: TLS cipher suite scanning, SSL certificate metadata parsing for subdomain discovery.
443.exploit: OWASP Top 10 application exploits executed inside encrypted TLS streams.
443.objective: Initial Access, Encrypted Command Execution, Data Exfiltration.
443.detection: Application vulnerabilities triggered within active encrypted sessions.

445.name: SMB
445.description: Server Message Block (SMB) is a network file sharing protocol facilitating access to shared files, printers, and ports.
445.whyAttackersCare: SMB is the primary lateral movement channel inside Active Directory domains. It supports admin share writing, remote commands execution, and hash harvesting.
445.recon: SMBv1 configuration scans, null session checks, admin share listing (C$, ADMIN$).
445.exploit: EternalBlue (MS17-010), Pass-the-Hash execution, printer spooler exploits (PrintNightmare).
445.objective: Lateral Movement, Credential Access, Ransomware Propagation.
445.detection: SMB connections established across boundary router firewalls or between segregated subnets.

88.name: Kerberos
88.description: Kerberos is a ticket-based computer network authentication protocol providing cryptographic identity verification.
88.whyAttackersCare: Core identity mechanism of Windows Active Directory. Vulnerable to ticket extraction and offline cracking attacks.
88.recon: User account verification via validation queries.
88.exploit: Kerberoasting SPN tickets, AS-REP roasting, Golden/Silver ticket forgings.
88.detection: High volumes of Kerberos ticket requests utilizing legacy RC4 encryption.

389.name: LDAP
389.description: Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) is used to access and maintain distributed directory records.
389.whyAttackersCare: Hosts Active Directory credential maps. Open binders allow listing privileged domain administrators and target groups.
389.recon: Anonymous directory binding, target name and email querying.
389.exploit: LDAP relay injections, pass-through authentication exploits.
389.detection: High volume of LDAP query structures originating from generic non-admin user endpoints.

1433.name: MSSQL
1433.description: Microsoft SQL Server is an enterprise relational database management engine.
1433.whyAttackersCare: Includes the powerful xp_cmdshell command interface, which allows database administrators to run direct OS commands.
1433.exploit: SA (sysadmin) account brute-force attacks, xp_cmdshell execution to spawn shells.
1433.objective: Privilege Escalation, Host Command Execution, Data Exfiltration.
1433.detection: Operating system command shells (cmd/powershell) spawned directly by sqlservr.exe.

3306.name: MySQL
3306.description: MySQL is an open-source relational database management system.
3306.whyAttackersCare: Hosts sensitive application backend tables. Root compromise allows database extraction and writing backdoors to the filesystem.
3306.recon: Banner version grabbing, default database configurations verification.
3306.exploit: Root account brute-forcing, SELECT INTO OUTFILE injection to write Web Shell scripts.
3306.objective: Data Exfiltration, Credential Access, Local Host Pivot.
3306.detection: High frequency of SQL syntax connection errors or failed login attempts on port 3306.

5432.name: PostgreSQL
5432.description: PostgreSQL is a powerful, open-source object-relational database system.
5432.whyAttackersCare: Permissive default configurations expose direct databases. Vulnerable to remote command execute exploits.
5432.exploit: Password dictionary attacks, COPY FROM PROGRAM executions to run host commands.
5432.objective: Data Extraction, Host Command Execution, Persistence.
5432.detection: Unencrypted database connection requests bypassing local app proxy servers.

5900.name: VNC
5900.description: Virtual Network Computing (VNC) is a graphical desktop sharing protocol for remote interface management.
5900.whyAttackersCare: Provides interactive mouse and keyboard display access. VNC setups often use short, weak passwords that are vulnerable to automated cracking.
5900.recon: Negotiation scans to verify auth requirements (check for 'None' security).
5900.exploit: VNC password dictionary cracking, session hijack exploits.
5900.detection: Multiple rapid graphical connection failures on Port 5900.

3389.name: RDP
3389.description: Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) is a proprietary graphical desktop management protocol developed by Microsoft.
3389.whyAttackersCare: Primary target for ransomware groups. Open RDP ports allow attackers to establish interactive logins and deploy malware host-wide.
3389.recon: Screen resolution query checks, host NLA support verification.
3389.exploit: Interactive credential guessing/spraying, exploiting CVEs like BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708).
3389.objective: Initial Access, System Takeover, Lateral Movement.
3389.detection: High volume of Event ID 4625 login failures registered on RDP port.

5985.name: WinRM
5985.description: Windows Remote Management (WinRM) is a SOAP-based protocol used for remote system administration.
5985.whyAttackersCare: WinRM is the backend for PowerShell Remoting. Access to WinRM allows executing PowerShell scripts with administrator rights for lateral shifts.
5985.exploit: Spraying local admin accounts, running Pass-the-Hash scripts over PowerShell Remoting.
5985.detection: WinRM traffic crossing firewall boundaries or originating from standard developer workstations.

5986.name: WinRM Secure
5986.description: Windows Remote Management (WinRM) encrypted over HTTPS.
5986.whyAttackersCare: Same remote administration privileges as standard WinRM, but encrypted, shielding payloads from boundary network IDS.
5986.exploit: Credential spraying, executing administrative scripts over SSL remote shell.
5986.detection: Encrypted remote admin logins crossing internal networks.
